coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient degradation and detoxification of antibiotics over C–Dots/siderite heteroaggregates in photo-Fenton systems: The crucial role of C–Dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yu–Hang
Long, Hao–Ran
Fang, Han–Liang
Wei, Lu–Ying
Cai, Feng–Ying
Rensing, Christopher
Zhong, Zhou
Lü, Jian
coredata.dc:description: The substantial presence of antibiotics in aquatic environments remains as a critical environmental issue that needs to be urgently addressed. In this study, mimetics of C–Dots/siderite heteroaggregates (CSI) were studied in a visible-light-responsive Fenton system as a platform for the degradation of antibiotics in contaminated water. By virtue of the excellent electron transfer of C–Dots, the sustained conversion cycles of Fe(II) → Fe(III) → Fe(II) in CSI were greatly accelerated, and meanwhile the H2O2 utilization was enhanced. The optimized CSI–3 nanocomposite displayed a prominent degradation efficiency towards a series of tetracycline analogs at ppb levels. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicated the excellent photo-Fenton catalytic performance of CSI originated from the increased d-band center and electron density, which considerably improves the utilization of H2O2. Both experimental and theoretical studies revealed that the correlated toxicity of degradation intermediates was significantly decreased. Additionally, a continuous flow device integrating the CSI photo-Fenton system maintained a high degradation efficiency after long-term treatment of simulated chlortetracycline wastewater. The presented work thereby confirms a spontaneous remediation process for the decontamination and detoxification of persistent organic pollutants under the action of heteroaggregates formed by engineered nanoparticles and natural minerals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Siderite
Carbon quantum dot
Tetracycline
Catalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and functional optimization of CSI
Mechanistic analysis of the Photo-Fenton system with CSI
Identification of intermediates and proposed degradation pathways
Practical application of CSI in a continuous flow reactor

Z. thanks the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (Grant No. 2021J05029). We would also like to acknowledge the technical support from Instrumental Analysis Center of Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of SID (a) and CSI (b); (c) HR-TEM and SAED (d) images of CSI; (e) Raman spectra of SID and CSI; high-resolution XPS spectrum of Fe 2p (f), O 1 s (g), C 1 s (h) of SID and CSI.

(a) The CTC degradation efficiency of SID and CSI under different conditions; (b) the TOC removal rate of CTC; (c) utilization rate of H2O2 ([catalyst] = 0.3 g/L, [pollutants] = 10 mg/L, [H2O2] = 5.0 mM); (d) H2O2 concentrations; (e) various catalyst dosages; (f) initial CTC concentrations; (g) different ions and humic acid ([Cl−] = [SO4 2−] = [CO3 2−] = 1.0 mM and [HA] = 5.0 mg/L); (h) different water matrices; and (i) tetracycline analogs.

(a) The Fe(II) concentration and Fe(II):Total iron in SID and CSI; (b) XPS spectra for Fe 2p of SID and CSI after photo-Fenton reactions; (c) transient photocurrent response under visible irradiation; (d) EIS Nyquist plots; (e) cyclic voltammetry curves; (f) Chronoamperometry curves with continuous addition of light and H2O2; (g) ESR spectra of DMPO−•O2 −, TEMP−1O2, DMPO−•OH and TEMPO−h+ with CSI; PDOS of (h) SID and (i) CSI.

(a) The CTC degradation efficiency of CSI and corresponding inhibition rate of E. coli growth; (b) growth curves of E. coli co-cultured with different solutions; (c) acute toxicity and (d) mutagenicity of CTC and degradation intermediates.

(a) Photograph of the continuous flow reactor; and (b) CTC degradation and total iron concentration in the reactor. Reaction conditions: [catalyst] = 0.1 g, [pollutants] = 10 mg/L, [H2O2] = 5.0 mM, [flow rate] = 70 mL h−1.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient degradation and detoxification of antibiotics over C–Dots/siderite heteroaggregates in photo-Fenton systems: The crucial role of C–Dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Soil Environmental Health and Regulation, College of Resources and Environment, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Soil Environmental Health and Regulation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Soil Environmental Health and Regulation, College of Resources and Environment, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, Fuzhou 350002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The substantial presence of antibiotics in aquatic environments remains as a critical environmental issue that needs to be urgently addressed. In this study, mimetics of C–Dots/siderite heteroaggregates (CSI) were studied in a visible-light-responsive Fenton system as a platform for the degradation of antibiotics in contaminated water. By virtue of the excellent electron transfer of C–Dots, the sustained conversion cycles of Fe(II) → Fe(III) → Fe(II) in CSI were greatly accelerated, and meanwhile the H2O2 utilization was enhanced. The optimized CSI–3 nanocomposite displayed a prominent degradation efficiency towards a series of tetracycline analogs at ppb levels. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicated the excellent photo-Fenton catalytic performance of CSI originated from the increased d-band center and electron density, which considerably improves the utilization of H2O2. Both experimental and theoretical studies revealed that the correlated toxicity of degradation intermediates was significantly decreased. Additionally, a continuous flow device integrating the CSI photo-Fenton system maintained a high degradation efficiency after long-term treatment of simulated chlortetracycline wastewater. The presented work thereby confirms a spontaneous remediation process for the decontamination and detoxification of persistent organic pollutants under the action of heteroaggregates formed by engineered nanoparticles and natural minerals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics, as vital pharmaceuticals for human and veterinary medication, have recently attracted significant attention in environmental safety [1], and have become an emerging concern with the widespread detection of antibiotic pollutants in various environments [2,3]. Antibiotics possess biotoxicity, environmental persistence, and bioaccumulation potential, which have been shown to cause adverse effects on both ecosystems and human health [4]. More importantly, the misuse and improper disposal of antibiotics has accelerated the emergence and dissemination of antibiotic resistant bacteria and antibiotic resistance genes in the environment [5,6], which further impairs the human capacity of disease treatment and public health security [7].

Conventional wastewater treatment techniques are limited in their capacities to fully mineralize antibiotics, leaving a significant portion of unconverted or even more toxic intermediate compounds [8]. Consequently, there is a pressing demand for an innovative technology capable of efficiently removing antibiotics while simultaneously detoxifying the contaminants. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been applied in wastewater treatment due to their rapid oxidation rate, extensive applicability, and high mineralization efficiency toward persistent organic pollutants [9–11]. It is noteworthy that Fenton reactions, as a class of highly efficient AOP technologies, are widely employed for the degradation and detoxification of antibiotics in aqueous phases [12–15]. However, iron-containing catalysts used to initiate heterogeneous Fenton reactions are primarily composed of trivalent iron [16,17], which typically results in a lower utilization rate of hydrogen peroxide for radical generation, thereby significantly affecting the efficiency of Fenton systems. Siderite (SID; FeCO3) as a natural mineral has attracted considerable attention in various fields, including heavy metal adsorption [18], arsenite removal [19], and lithium storage. It is superior in that the abundant Fe(II) species in siderites can be effectively utilized to propel Fenton reactions [20,21], thereby promoting the generation of reactive species. Nevertheless, inefficient transformation of Fe(II) → Fe(III) → Fe(II) limits the sustainability of heterogeneous Fenton reactions as soon as Fe(II) species in siderite are fully converted [22,23]. To address this issue, surface modifications of siderite to achieve rapid Fe(III) reduction via electron transfer at the interface may offer an effective solution.

Carbon quantum dots (C–Dots) are a fascinating class of nanostructured carbons widely being employed in the field of membrane technology and photocatalysis due to their unique physicochemical properties such as ultra–small sizes, excellent biocompatibility, rich surface functional groups, tunable hydrophilicity and antifouling characteristics [24–26]. In particular, C–Dots are able to serve as both electron donors and acceptors, thereby facilitating the transfer of photoinduced charge carriers at the surface/interface. In a photochemical system, the incorporation of C–Dots as “artificial antennas” indues the absorption and conversion of photons with low energy, by which more efficient photoelectron transfer from Fe(III) to Fe(II) is expected to improve the overall performance and sustainability of photo-Fenton systems under broad-spectrum irradiation [27]. Upon the formation of ·OH radicals over iron minerals [28], interfacial interactions in the heteroaggregates of C–Dots and SID may potentially enhance the capacity to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) for the removal of organic pollutants through photochemical oxidation processes.

In recent years, the release of synthetic nanoparticles (NPs) into the environment has significantly increased with an extensive application of nanotechnologies in commercial and industrial products [29,30]. In this context, heteroaggregates of NPs with non-biological substances are ubiquitously observed in natural environmental systems including soils, waters, and sediments [31]. Although the occurrence and transport of these heteroaggregates have previously been established through the interpretation of influential factors i.e. pH, ionic strength, and natural organic matter [32], more detailed and in-depth studies on the behavior and physicochemical property of heteroaggregates in relation to environmental pollutants and their remediation remain relatively scarce. Especially, heteroaggregates of ultra-small inorganic nanoparticles (sub 5 nm) and minerals, formed via combined actions of adsorption and repulsion, have shown miscellaneous quantum tunneling effects, intermolecular charge transfer, and specific adsorption generated by the mutual coupling of molecules at the interface. These characteristics of heteroaggregates are believed to be closely interlinked with the migration (fixation and diffusion), elimination, and fate of exogenous pollutants in multiple environmental matrices.

Inspired by the phenomenon of heteroaggregation in natural minerals and engineered nanomaterials, mimetics of C–Dots/siderite heteroaggregates (CSI) were herein studied towards the degradation of a series of tetracycline analogs via photo-assisted heterogeneous Fenton processes, as well as to investigate the pivotal role of C–Dots in CSI. Structural features and photocatalytic activity of CSI heteroaggregates were systematically investigated through experimental and analytical characterizations. With an optimal C–Dots loading, the CSI–3 exhibited a degradation kinetic 2.8 times higher than that of the pristine siderite towards a representative chlortetracycline (CTC). The enhanced photo-Fenton activity in this current system was ascribed to the improved separation of charge carriers and the tuned band structure in CSI. An underlying mechanism was proposed based on the fragmental identification and cytotoxic evaluation of degradation intermediates of CTC. Moreover, density functional theory (DFT) calculations successfully predicted that the d-band center of CSI shifted forward to the Fermi level, which endowed stronger adsorption capacity and higher electron transfer rate. This work thus provides new insights into the contributions of mineral/nanomaterial heteroaggregates on the environmental fate of tetracycline antibiotics, as well as the potentials of these heteroaggregates for efficient degradation and detoxification of antibiotics.

Structural properties of the heteroaggregates of carbon quantum dots and siderite were tested by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution microscopy (HR-TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray energy spectrum analysis (XPS), UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), Raman spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), electron spin-resonance spectroscopy (ESR) etc. Details of instrumental characterizations are provided in the Supporting Information.

Stock solutions of C–Dots (120 g L–1) were first prepared via a simple method documented in the literature [25]. For the synthesis of CSI, 0.81 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 3.0 g urea were added to 70 mL deionized water, and then stirred for 10 min to form a homogeneous dispersion. Afterwards, 0.8 g C6H8O6 and a certain amount of C–Dots stock solution was introduced to the above solution, followed by ultrasonication for 30 min. The above mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclaves and heated at 160℃ for 3 h, and the resulting precipitate was washed several times with deionized water and dried under vacuum at 60℃. A series of nanocomposites with various C–Dots loadings were prepared via controlling the volume (V) of C–Dots stock solutions and denoted as CSI–x (x = 1, V = 100 μL; x = 2, V = 300 μL; x = 3, V = 500 μL; and x = 4, V = 700 μL). Referencing siderite was prepared in a similar process without the addition of C–Dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and functional optimization of CSI
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-Fenton performances of different CSI was tested, as shown in Fig. S1. CSI–x series nanocomposites exhibited significantly enhanced photo-Fenton activity towards CTC degradation (over 95 %) in comparison with the pristine SID (72.3 %) and C–Dots (45.1 %), of which the CSI–3 demonstrated the highest reaction rate (k) of 0.09638 min−1, accounting for 2.8 and 6.5 times that of SID and C–Dots respectively (Fig. S1b). Hence, the CSI–3 was selected as an optimal presentative heteroaggregate, denoted as CSI hereafter, for subsequent characterizations and experimental studies.

The catalytic activity of C–Dots, SID and CSI catalyst was investigated by CTC degradation in a photo-Fenton system. Typically, 15 mg of catalyst was added into a 50 mL quartz vessel with 50 mL of CTC solution. The mixture was stirred in the dark at 500 rpm for 30 min to achieve the adsorption equilibrium. Subsequently, 5.0 mM H2O2 was introduced, and a 300 W Xenon lamp with a cut-off filter (λ ≥ 400 nm, light intensity = 400 mW/cm2) was applied thereafter. To maintain a constant temperature of approximately 25 °C, the reactor was connected to a circulating water system during the experiment. Afterwards, 2.0 mL of suspension was periodically extracted, filtered using a 0.22 μm filter tip, and quenched with excessive methanol to remove the reactive oxygen species. Then the residual concentration of CTC was measured by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and the CTC degradation efficiency was calculated as D = C/C 0 × 100 %, where C and C0 are the concentrations substituted into the standard curve before and after the reaction, respectively.

Escherichia coli (E. coli) was chosen as an experimental organism due to its high sensitivity to CTC [33]. The E. coli strain (CCTCCAB93154) was provided by the China Centre for Type Culture Collection. All experiments were conducted under sterile conditions. The liquid culture medium consisted of a mixture of yeast extract, peptone, sodium chloride, CTC, and bacterial suspension. The medium was first incubated in a bacterial culture chamber at 37 °C, and 1.0 mL of the solution was then extracted into a centrifuge tube every hour. The OD600 was measured using a UV-vis spectrophotometer, and the growth curve of E. coli was recorded. The inhibition rate was calculated with the equation of R = (L0 − Lt)/L0 × 100 % where L0 and Lt are OD600 of bacteria cultured in sterile water and treated CTC solution, respectively.

To further elucidate the origin of the excellent performance of CSI, DFT calculations were performed by using Vienna ab-initio simulation package (VASP). The computational details are included in Text S8.

Surface morphologies and microstructures of SID and CSI were studied by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. S2, SID displayed typical hollow microspheres with diameters ranging from 1.0 to 2.0 μm assembled by secondary nanostructures (Fig. 1 a). Incorporation of C–Dots induced a gradual transformation of the hollow microspheres of siderite, leading to the formation of spherical microstructures with a diameter of approximately 1.0 μm (Fig. 1b). This was attributed to the heteroaggregation of siderite and C–Dots, where the latter served as templates and resulted in the replacement of original hollow microspheres. As indicated in the HR-TEM image depicted in Fig. 1c and 1d, as-–prepared CSI nanocomposites exhibited exceptional crystallinity with an interplanar spacing of approximately 0.254 nm, corresponding to the (006) crystallographic plane of siderite [34]. Moreover, the presence of a 0.22 nm interplanar spacing was assigned to the (100) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon, suggesting the presence of C–Dots in nanocomposites [25].

XRD patterns of SID and CSI were analyzed (Fig. S3) and the diffraction peaks at 2θ 24.76°, 31.99°, 34.97°, 38.33°, 42.32°, 46.16°, 50.77° and 52.83° were attributed to the characteristics of siderite (FeCO3; PDF #29 − 0696). However, the presence of C–Dots was unable to be identified in XRD due to their low crystallinity and abundance. In the Raman spectra (Fig. 1e), the characteristic peaks at 1083 cm−1 observed for both SID and CSI were attributed to the symmetrical stretching vibration (ʋ 1) of carbonates [35], whereas a distinctive Raman peak at 1300 cm−1 was assigned to the d-band disordered sp2 carbon in C–Dots [36]. Zeta potential measurements indicated that surfaces of dispersive SID (6.9 mV) and C–Dots (−10.0 mV) were oppositely charged at pH 2.5 (the synthetic condition) (Fig. S4a), which was favorable for the heteroaggregation of these counterparts to form CSI nanocomposites via electrostatic interactions.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to verify the elemental composition of SID and CSI. The XPS survey spectra indicated the presence of Fe, O and C (Fig. S4b). In the Fe 2p spectra (Fig. 1f), characteristics at 710.43 eV for SID and 710.36 eV for CSI corresponded to Fe2+, indicating a predominant presence of Fe2+. The O 1 s spectra of SID and CSI exhibited two characteristics including surface adsorbed oxygen species (Oads) and lattice oxygen (Olatt) (Fig. 1g).

The decrease in lattice oxygen intensity of CSI indicated the disruption of crystal structure, which can activate lattice oxygen to promote the release of ROS [37]. Of note, the shifts of binding energies observed for CSI was attributed to the formation of Fe–O–C bonds upon the addition of C–Dots [38]. Moreover, the photogenerated electrons can be quickly transferred to the SID via Fe–O–C bonds for Fe(III) reduction under visible irradiation, and thus to promote the production of ROS. In the C 1 s peaks of SID and CSI (Fig. 1h), characteristics of C–O bonds was extensively observed [39]. Additionally, the C–O characteristic of CSI decreased to 286.01 eV due to the introduction of π bonds from C–Dots, which increased the local electron density around the C–O bonds [40].

The photo-Fenton performance of SID and CSI was extensively investigated in various systems. Before catalysis experiments, the adsorption performance of catalysts was investigated (Fig. S1c) CSI generally showed stronger adsorption capacity, indicating pollutants can be oxidized and degraded more quickly by ROS on the surface of catalysts. In addition, it was observed that the removal efficiency of chlortetracycline (CTC) in either photocatalytic or Fenton system was significantly lower than that of the photo-Fenton system (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S5a), indicating the presence of a synergistic effect between photocatalysis and Fenton processes [41]. Due to the incorporation of photo-responsive C–Dots, the CSI exhibited much higher rates of CTC degradation (96.1 %, 40 min; Fig. 2a), TOC removal (63.4 %, 40 min; Fig. 2b) and H2O2 utilization (61.1 %, 40 min; Fig. 2c) in comparison with those of the SID. Moreover, the CSI could preserve over 94 % of the initial catalytic ability for CTC removal after five consecutive runs (Fig. S6a), and meanwhile the iron leaching in each run ranged from 0.1 mg L −1 to 0.55 mg/L, which accounted for approximately 0.7 % of catalyst loss. The XRD of CSI before and after cycling reactions were nearly identical (Fig. S6b), indicating an excellent reusability and structural stability of the CSI.

To comprehensively assess the potential of CSI towards CTC degradation, photo-Fenton performances were systematically examined under different reaction processes (Fig. 2). The effect of H2O2 concentrations was first examined (Fig. 2d). It could be shown that a low H2O2 concentration (1 mM) resulted in considerably reduced CTC degradation efficiency. Furthermore, an overdose of H2O2 (10 mM) also inhibited the CTC degradation, since ·OH was potentially consumed by excessive H2O2 [42]. An increase of catalyst dosage from 0.1 g/L to 0.3 g/L generally enhanced the reaction rate (k) to ca. 0.09638 min−1 (Fig. S5b), and a further increase of catalyst dosage only caused insignificant changes. These results suggested that the optimization of H2O2 concentration and catalyst dosage might provide an ideal amount of reactive oxygen species and reaction sites, thereby demonstrating a higher efficiency of CTC degradation. Moreover, the CTC degradation efficiency was steadily maintained at a high level (over 95 %) with initial CTC concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 10 mg/L (Fig. 2f), suggesting an excellent performance of CSI towards CTC degradation. The effect of pH is considered crucial in versatile advanced oxidation processes, and it is therefore investigated in this current photo-Fenton system (Fig. S7). The CTC degradation efficiency of CSI remained relatively stable under acidic conditions due to a relatively higher oxidation capability of ·OH, however, the CSI was somewhat passivated under neutral conditions.

Considering the complex composition of natural water, the influence of various anions and organic matters was also examined (Fig. 2g). Results showed that the presence of anions (Cl− and SO4 2−) and humic acid only insignificant affected the CTC degradation efficiency. However, CO3 2− notably retarded the capacity of CSI towards CTC degradation. This could be attributed to the reaction between CO3 2− and ·OH, as well as the increase in solution pH [43]. Moreover, the photo-Fenton performances of CSI were evaluated under different water environment (Fig. 2h). It is noteworthy that CTC was efficiently removed within 40 min (exceeding 89.1 %) even in natural lake water and breeding water containing a large amount of impurities, indicating potential applications in practical scenarios. To further test the universality of CSI, other tetracycline antibiotics including tetracycline, doxycycline and oxytetracycline were selected as targeted pollutants. Under optimized reaction conditions, the CSI generally showed high degradation efficiency towards the tetracycline series antibiotics (over 95 %) in 40 min, likely owing to their structural similarity (Fig. 2i). The slightly different degradation rate can be attributed to the different molecular weight of tetracycline analogs (CTC has the largest molecular weight) and local changes on the substituents of fragments that cause minor difference of bonding energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic analysis of the Photo-Fenton system with CSI
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the valence state and conversion of iron species in the photo-Fenton system, concentrations of iron ions in the solutions were systematically monitored. Notably, the solution concentration of Fe(II) considerably increased after 20 min (Fig. S8a) due to the exceptional electron transfer ability of C–Dots, which efficiently expedited the migration of photogenerated electrons, thereby significantly accelerating the conversion of Fe(III) to Fe(II). In addition, the release of Fe2+ showed a minor impact on the degradation of CTC (Fig. S8b), indicating that the CSI/H2O2/Vis system primarily relied on heterogeneous Fenton reaction. Subsequently, the generation of Fe(II) in CSI was further substantiated through assessment of the valence states of iron species in catalysts before and after the reaction (Fig. 3 a). The Fe (II)/Total iron ratio in both catalysts prior to the reaction were approximately 1:1, which was consistent with the XPS analyses (Fig. 1f). Moreover, the decrease of the Fe(II)/Total iron ratio of CSI after photo-Fenton reactions was less significant compared to that of the SID. This discrepancy was attributed to the enhanced electron transfer within the system with CSI, in which the C–Dots acted as both interfacial electron shuttles for boosting the Fe(III)-to-Fe(II) conversion. The Fe 2p spectra demonstrated that the recycled CSI contained a higher Fe(II) content at the surface than the SID (Fig. 3b), indicating a substantial reduction of Fe(III) to regenerate Fe(II). These results provided further evidence that the excellent electron transfer capacity of C–Dots accelerated the sustained conversion of Fe(III) to Fe(II) in the photo-Fenton process.

The mechanisms of electron transfer in catalysts were investigated through comprehensive analysis of optical and electrochemical measurements. As indicated by the UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), both SID and CSI showed strong light absorption in the visible light region (Fig. S9a) [28]. The bandgap energy was calculated with the Tauc equation (Text S7), which revealed bandgap energies of 1.64 V and 1.48 V for SID and CSI, respectively. The reduced bandgap energy of CSI was attributable to the introduction of additional electronic energy levels induced by C–Dots. Mott-Schottky plots indicated the flat band potentials of SID and CSI were ca. − 0.40 V vs. Ag/AgCl and ca. − 0.45 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively (Fig. S9). The more negative flat band potential of CSI indicated a higher charge carrier density [44]. Furthermore, the CSI demonstrated a substantially superior photocurrent response in comparison to that of the SID (Fig. 3c), which indicated a noteworthy charge separation efficiency in CSI. Additionally, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of SID and CSI was recorded (Fig. 3d), in which the CSI exhibited a smaller Nyquist curve radius, indicating lower resistance and faster electron transfer rate. In the photoluminescence spectrum (PL) recorded by the photoluminescence spectrometer FLS1000 (Edinburgh Instruments), a decreased fluorescence signal was observed in CSI (Fig. S9d) due to a lower electron-hole recombination rate, which is responsible for the production of more photocarriers and the enhancement in the of photo-Fenton activity.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was also conducted to assess the electron transfer rate at the interface of catalysts (Fig. 3e). Of note, the cathodic (ipc) and anodic (ipa) peak currents of CSI were considerably higher than those of the SID, suggesting a higher interface electron transfer rate and stronger redox capability in the CSI. It was rational that the introduction of C–Dots into SID significantly accelerated the transfer of electrons within the system, thereby promoting the regeneration of Fe(II) from Fe(III). Chronoamperometry was applied to examine the electron transfer rates between the catalyst and H2O2 (Fig. 3f). Under visible irradiation, the addition of H2O2 resulted in a significant increase in the current density of CSI in comparison with that of the SID, indicating a more efficient electron transfer from the catalyst to H2O2. The sharp increase of current density observed for CSI further demonstrated its superior ability to activate H2O2.

The generation of active oxygen species in the system of CSI/H2O2/vis was verified through radical scavenging experiments (Fig. S10). TBA, TEMPO, AO, and FFA were utilized as quenchers for ·OH, ·O2 −, h+, and 1O2, respectively. The addition of TBA, TEMPO, AO, and FFA caused significant decreases in the CTC degradation efficiency of 65.8 %, 55.6 %, 63.3 %, and 59.5 %, respectively. This indicated that active oxygen species and holes all contributed to the oxidative degradation of CTC. Moreover, ESR signals of 1:1:1:1 (DMPO−·O2 −), 1:1:1 (Temp−1O2), 1:2:2:1 (DMPO−·OH), and 1:1:1 (TEMPO−h+) were clearly observed in the reliable generation of ·O2 −, 1O2, ·OH and h+ during photo-Fenton reactions (Fig. 3g). Notably, a significant increase in the signal intensity of 1O2 was observed likely due to the general conversion pathway of 1O2 formation (O2→•O2 −→1O2).

By means of theoretical calculations on the density of electron-rubbing states (DOS), the electronic structures of catalysts were studied, coupled with analyzing the physical mechanisms correlated to the strong adsorption of H2O2 on the catalyst surface (Fig. 3h and 3i). The optimized geometric structures of model catalysts SID and CSI are shown in Fig. S11. A band gap was observed near the fermi level in both spin channel of SID, suggesting that the conductivity is limited. For CSI, the state of introduced C–Dots were distributed at broad energy range. These results suggest that the chemical adsorption of C–Dots is positioned, meanwhile charge exchange occurs between the C–Dots and SID counterparts. Furthermore, such phenomenon causes the redistribution of the Fe−3d orbital and the band gap in both spin channels will have disappeared. This indicates that the adsorption of C–Dots will improve the conductivity of SID, which consequently enhance the adsorption of H2O2. Additionally, the positive shift in the d-band-center of CSI suggests enhanced adsorption capacity at the Fe sites. The reduction in the gap between the d-band-center and Fermi energy level, coupled with a more discrete distribution of d orbitals near the Fermi energy level in CSI, implies a higher rate of charge transfer at the reaction interface [45]. This generally results in lower free energy barriers and activation energy, thereby significantly boosting the utilization efficiency of H2O2 [46,47].

Based on the above analysis and characterization, a possible mechanism for photo-Fenton degradation of CTC with the CSI has been proposed: Fe(II) on the surface of CSI reacts with H2O2 and initiates the Fenton process by generating ·OH radicals; subsequently, photogenerated electrons produced under visible irradiation are rapidly captured by C–Dots and then being transfered to the catalyst surface to participate in the formation of various reactive species (i.e. •O2 − and 1O2) in the presence of oxygen, thereby boosting the photo-Fenton activity of CSI; meanwhile, photogenerated electrons are also involved in the conversion of Fe(III) to Fe(II) to realize a sustained chain reaction of free radicals; ultimately, multiple reactive oxygen species and photogenerated holes collectively contributed to the oxidative degradation of CTC. The specific reaction pathways are as follows:
(1) Fe(II) + H2O2 → •OH + Fe(III) + OH−
(2) CSI + hʋ → h+ + e−
(3) C–Dots + e− → C–Dots (e−)
(4) O2 + C–Dots (e−) → •O2 − + C–Dots
(5) •O2 − + •OH → 1O2 + HO−
(6) C–Dots (e−) + Fe(III) → C–Dots + Fe(II)
(7) •O2 − + •OH + 1O2 + h++ CTC → H2O + CO2 + intermediates

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of intermediates and proposed degradation pathways
ce:section.ce:para: The potential degradation pathways of CTC during the photo-Fenton process were unveiled by means of LC-MS and total ion chromatograms spectra (Fig. S12 and S13). The peak observed at a retention time of approximately 11.25 min was assigned to the CTC molecule, which decreased significantly during the photo-Fenton reactions, indicating the effective degradation of CTC. Three potential degradation pathways were proposed based on the identification of fourteen degradation intermediates (Fig. 4 ). In pathway 1, CTC first underwent dechlorination to generate intermediate P1 (m/z = 444); subsequently, intermediate P2 (m/z = 398) was formed through dehydroxylation and decarbonylation; P3 (m/z = 309) was further generated through demethylation and dihydroxylation; afterwards, P4 (m/z = 266) and P5 (m/z = 195) were produced through amide cleavage and ring-opening reactions. In pathway 2, the CTC molecule was transformed through dechlorination, dehydroxylation, and demethylation to generate intermediate P6 (m/z = 415) that further underwent dehydroxylation to produce P7 (m/z = 384); P8 (m/z = 311) was then generated through amide cleavage and dihydroxylation; intermediates P9 (m/z = 258) and P10 (m/z = 177) were produced through ring opening and demethylation. In pathway 3, CTC molecules experienced C−N bond cleavage, demethylation, and deamidation to generate intermediate P11 (m/z = 381), which was subsequently involved in transformation via dechlorination and ROS oxidation to produce P12 (m/z = 351) and P13 (m/z = 271); P14 (m/z = 185) was generated thereafter by ring-opening reactions. Finally, most of these degradation intermediates were mineralized into inorganic salts, carbon dioxide and water [48–51].

In light of the potential generation of more toxic intermediates by contaminants during the degradation process [52], co-cultivation experiments were conducted with E. coli, as the model bacterium, using various untreated and treated CTC solutions to elucidate the toxicity of degradation intermediates. Despite the considerable CTC degradation within the initial 10 min, the treated CTC solution exhibited noticeable inhibitory effects on the growth of E. coli (Fig. 5 a). In contrast, the E. coli inhibition rate was significantly declined after 20 min, which agrees with the disappearance of characteristics of CTC in the mass spectrum (Fig. S12). Furthermore, the growth of E. coli in untreated CTC solution displayed a continuously low OD600 level (Fig. 5b). However, E. coli growth in treated CTC solution and sterile water (CK) exhibited a nearly identical OD600, indicating an insignificant toxicity of intermediates produced during photo-Fenton CTC degradation.

Toxicity of the degradation intermediates were further evaluated by using the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) based on the quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) model to install a series of parameters related to toxicity, including acute toxicity (Fathead minnow LC50 (96 h)) and mutagenicity. For LC50, there are four toxicity levels: highly toxic (acute toxicity < 1), toxic (1 < acute toxicity < 10), harmful (10 < acute toxicity < 100), and low toxicity (100 < acute toxicity). Mutagenicity is classified as positive or negative. In comparison with the LC50 of CTC, most intermediate products exhibited a decrease in toxicity (Fig. 5 and Table S1), except for P7, P8, P4, and P3 identified as more toxic intermediates. Specifically, the toxicity of the final products (P5, P10, P14) was significantly decreased. Although most intermediate products remained in a state of positive mutagenicity as the parent CTC, the low molecular weight intermediates generally exhibited negative mutagenicity. Overall, the toxicity of CTC decomposition products is considerably reduced, and the photo-Fenton system of CSI has demonstrated high efficiency in reducing the environmental risks of persistent organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of CSI in a continuous flow reactor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential practical applications of the CSI photo-Fenton system, a continuous flow reactor containing 0.1 g CSI was constructed to treat the simulated CTC wastewater (Fig. 6 a and S15). As shown in Fig. 6b, the degradation efficiency of CTC in the flow reactor was approximately 54.1 % within the initial 30 min, however, the CTC degradation in the continuous flow system gradually increased to over 90 % with the progression of treatment. This was attributed to the prolonged retention time of CTC solution in the reactor, thereby resulting in the accumulation of additional reactive oxygen species. Moreover, the photo-Fenton flow reactor exhibited a low iron leaching (0.2 to 0.3 mg/L) after operating for 7 h, demonstrating an excellent catalytic stability. These results indicated that CSI heteroaggregates are promising candidates toward more practically environmental applications for the degradation of tetracycline antibiotics.

In summary, a class of novel nanocomposite materials was synthesized by incorporating carbon quantum dots (C–Dots) into siderite (SID). Upon the addition of C–Dots, the catalytic activity of the photo-Fenton systems was significantly improved, thereby displaying excellent performances towards CTC degradation and enhanced removal efficiency of total organic carbon (TOC). Through electrochemical analysis and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the underlying mechanism of optimized Fenton reaction activity by C–Dots was proposed. The enhancement primarily relies on the unique electron transfer capability of C–Dots, which accelerates the formation of Fe(II) and enhances the utilization efficiency of H2O2. Importantly, related toxicity of degradation intermediates of chlortetracycline (CTC) has been both experimentally and theoretically investigated, in which acute toxicity and mutagenicity have been found significantly decreased. Additionally, a constructed continuous flow reactor integrating the CSI photo-Fenton system exhibits a promising treatment efficiency towards simulated CTC wastewater. This study thus provides a new strategy for efficient degradation and detoxification of antibiotics.

Yu–Hang Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Hao–Ran Long: Investigation, Data curation. Han–Liang Fang: Methodology, Investigation. Lu–Ying Wei: Methodology. Feng–Ying Cai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Christopher Rensing: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Zhou Zhong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jian Lü: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: J. L. gratefully for acknowledges the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 22371042). Z. Z. thanks the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (Grant No. 2021J05029). We would also like to acknowledge the technical support from Instrumental Analysis Center of Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2024.127912.

ce:section.ce:para:
